text.title: C# ASP.NET Core + EF Core Failure Modes
text.summary: Four antipatterns observed in production .NET deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Controller method body inlines EF queries, validation, business logic. Hundreds of LOC per action; tests require full integration host.
antipattern.root-cause: Controller skipped service interface; persistence concerns leaked into transport layer.
antipattern.fix: Extract `I<Feature>Service`; controller becomes a thin dispatcher returning `ActionResult<TResponse>`. Slice test mocks the service.

antipattern.symptom: List endpoint returns `IQueryable<OrderEntity>` or full entities serialized via JSON. Lazy-loaded navigations trigger SQL during response writes; `DbContext` disposed before serialisation finishes.
antipattern.root-cause: Missing `.AsNoTracking()` + missing DTO projection; entities returned directly.
antipattern.fix: Project to a `record` DTO inside the query: `.AsNoTracking().Select(o => new OrderResponse(o.Id, o.Total))`. Materialize before return.

antipattern.symptom: Controller signature `public Task<OrderEntity> Get(int id)`. Persistence columns (`RowVersion`, `Discriminator`) leak into public API. Adding a column to the table breaks consumers.

root-cause.source: DDD layering + ASP.NET Core API guidance

antipattern.root-cause: Entity reused as transport DTO; no separation between persistence and API contracts.
antipattern.fix: Create `OrderResponse` `record` in a `Dtos/` folder. Map explicitly inside the service. Controller returns the DTO.

antipattern.symptom: Controller and service methods omit `CancellationToken ct`. Client disconnects mid-request, server keeps processing, EF queries continue. Throughput degraded.
antipattern.root-cause: Controller actions did not accept `CancellationToken`; service didn't propagate.
antipattern.fix: Every async controller action accepts `CancellationToken ct`. Every service method threads it through to EF (`ToListAsync(ct)`, `SaveChangesAsync(ct)`).
